text: The surgeon mobilizes the right colon to access the retroperitoneum, then identifies and follows the ureter proximally to the renal hilum. The two groups of vessels are dissected and isolated around the hilum.
The surgeon clips and divides the superior vein draining the tumor, followed by clipping and dividing the main stem and branches of the superior artery supplying the tumor.
Intravenous endocyanin green is administered, and fluorescence imaging reveals no blood flow to the tumor while showing excellent perfusion to the normal kidney tissue.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to identify the tumor margins. Ischemia is applied by clamping the inferior artery and vein, followed by sharp dissection to excise the tumor.

The surgeon completes the oversewing of the tumor bed and finishes the inner layer of phrenography. Early unclamping of the vessels is performed, resulting in a total warm ischemia time of eleven minutes.
The surgeon completes the outer layer of rhinography using interrupted sutures and a sliding clip technique, securing the tissue layers in place.

The surgeon completes final hemostasis by applying flow seal and tail seal to the surgical site. The excised specimen is placed into an endocatch bag for removal, and a GP drain is inserted into the surgical area.
